The    global attribute  is an  enumerated  attribute that indicates whether the element can be dragged, either with native browser behavior or the  HTML Drag and Drop API .

The   attribute may be applied to elements that strictly fall under the  HTML namespace , which means that it cannot be applied to  SVGs .

For more information about what namespace declarations look like, and what they do, see  Namespace crash course .

can have the following values:

: the element can be dragged.

: the element cannot be dragged.

Warning:

This attribute is  enumerated  and not  Boolean . A value of   or   is mandatory, and shorthand like   is forbidden. The correct usage is  .

If this attribute is not set, its default value is  , which means drag behavior is the default browser behavior: only text selections, images, and links can be dragged. For other elements, the event   must be set for drag and drop to work, as shown in this  comprehensive example .

The    global attribute

is an  enumerated  attribute defining what action label (or icon) to present for the enter key on virtual keyboards.

Try it

Description

Form controls  (such as

or   elements) or elements using

can specify an

attribute to control what kind of virtual keyboard

will be used. To further improve the user's experience, the enter key can be customized specifically by providing an

attribute indicating how the enter key should be labeled (or which icon should be shown). The enter key usually

If no   attribute is provided, the user agent might use contextual information from the

,

next ,  ⇥

Typically taking the user to the previous field that will accept text.

title: HTML attribute: fetchpriority
content:
HTML attribute: fetchpriority

Baseline

2024

Newly available

Since October 2024, this feature works across the latest devices and browser versions. This feature might not work in older devices or browsers.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The   attribute allows a developer to signal that fetching a particular image early in the loading process has more or less impact on user experience than a browser can reasonably infer when assigning an internal priority.

This in turn allows the browser to increase or decrease the priority, and potentially load the image earlier or later than it would otherwise.

This attribute may be applied to  ,  , and   elements. It also has an  SVG counterpart .

The fetch priority can be used to complement  preloading , allowing a developer to boost the priority ahead of less-impactful resources that have a higher default priority.

For example, if a developer knows that a particular image significantly contributes to the website's  Largest Contentful Paint  (LCP) they could add   for the image and then further boost the priority using the   attribute.

Note that both the internal priority of any fetch operation, and the impact of   on the priority, are entirely browser dependent.

This attribute is  enumerated  and can have one of the following values:

Fetches the external resource at a high priority relative to other external resources.

Fetches the external resource at a low priority relative to other external resources.

Doesn't set a preference for the fetch priority.

It is used if no value or an invalid value is set.

This is the default.

Usage notes

The attribute should be used sparingly, as excessive or incorrect prioritization can degrade performance.

title: <label>: The Label element
content:
<label>: The Label element

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since July 2015.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element represents a caption for an item in a user interface.

Try it

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

The value is the   of the  labelable  form control in the same document,  associating the   with that form control . Note that its JavaScript reflection property is  .

Usage notes

Associating a label with a form control

The first element in the document with an   attribute matching the value of the   attribute is the  labeled control  for this   element — if the element with that   is actually a  labelable element . If it is  not  a labelable element, then the   attribute has no effect. If there are other elements that also match the   value, later in the document, they are not considered.

Multiple   elements can be associated with the same form control by having multiple   elements with the same   attribute value, which gives the form control multiple labels.

Associating a   with a form control, such as   or   offers some major advantages:

The label text is not only visually associated with its corresponding text input; it is programmatically associated with it too. This means that, for example, a screen reader will read out the label when the user is focused on the form input, making it easier for an assistive technology user to understand what data should be entered.

When a user clicks or touches/taps a label, the browser passes the focus to its associated input (the resulting event is also raised for the input). That increased hit area for focusing the input provides an advantage to anyone trying to activate it — including those using a touch-screen device.

There are two ways to associate a   with a form control, commonly referred to as  explicit  and  implicit  association.

To explicitly associate a   element with an   element, you first need to add the   attribute to the   element. Next, you add the   attribute to the   element, where the value of   is the same as the   in the   element.

html

Alternatively, you can nest the   directly inside the  , in which case the   and   attributes are not needed because the association is implicit:

html

Note:

A   element can have both a   attribute and a contained control element, as long as the   attribute points to the contained control element.

These two methods are equivalent, but there are a few considerations:

While common browser and  screen reader  combinations support implicit association, not all assistive technologies do.

Depending on your design, the type of association may impact stylability. Making the   and form control sibling elements instead of parent-child means they are separate, adjacent boxes, enabling more customizable layout such as lining them up with grid or flex layout methods.

Explicit association requires the form control to have an  , which must be unique in the whole document. This is hard especially in a componentized application. Frameworks often provide their own solutions, such as React's  , but it still requires extra orchestration to get right.

Generally, we recommend using explicit association with the   attribute, to ensure compatibility with external tools and assistive technologies. In fact, you can simultaneously nest  and  provide  /  for maximum compatibility.

The form control that a label is labeling is called the  labeled control  of the label element. Multiple labels can be associated with the same form control:

html

Elements that can be associated with a   element include  ,   (except for  ),  ,  ,  ,   and  .

Accessibility

Interactive content

Other than the  implicitly associated  form control, don't place additional interactive elements such as  anchors  or  buttons  inside a  . Doing so makes it difficult for people to activate the form input associated with the  .

Don't do this:

html

Prefer this:

html

Note:

It is a good practice to place any necessary context, such as the link to the terms and conditions, before the form control, so that the user can read it before they interact with the control.

Headings

Placing  heading elements  within a   interferes with many kinds of assistive technology, because headings are commonly used as  a navigation aid . If the label's text needs to be adjusted visually, use CSS classes applied to the   element instead.

If a  form , or a section of a form needs a title, use the   element placed within a  .

Don't do this:

html

Prefer this:

html

Buttons

An   element with a   declaration and a valid   attribute does not need a label associated with it. Doing so may actually interfere with how assistive technology parses the button input. The same applies for the   element.

Examples

html

Defining an explicit label with the "for" attribute

html

title: <datalist>: The HTML Data List element
content:
<datalist>: The HTML Data List element

Limited availability

This feature is not Baseline because it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element contains a set of   elements that represent the permissible or recommended options available to choose from within other controls.

Try it

To bind the   element to the control, we give it a unique identifier in the   attribute, and then add the   attribute to the   element with the same identifier as value.

Only certain types of   support this behavior, and it can also vary from browser to browser.

Each   element should have a   attribute, which represents a suggestion to be entered into the input. It can also have a   attribute, or, missing that, some text content, which may be displayed by the browser instead of   (Firefox), or in addition to   (Chrome and Safari, as supplemental text). The exact content of the drop-down menu depends on the browser, but when clicked, content entered into control field will always come from the   attribute.

Note:

is not a replacement for  . A   does not represent an input itself; it is a list of suggested values for an associated control. The control can still accept any value that passes validation, even if it is not in this suggestion list.

Attributes

This element has no other attributes than the  global attributes , common to all elements.

Accessibility

When deciding to use the   element, here are some accessibility issues to be mindful of:

The font size of the data list's options does not zoom, always remaining the same size. The contents of the autosuggest do not grow or shrink when the rest of the contents are zoomed in or out.

As targeting the list of options with CSS is very limited to non-existent, rendering can not be styled for high-contrast mode.

Some screen reader/browser combinations, including NVDA and Firefox, do not announce the contents of the autosuggest popup.

Examples

Textual types

Recommended values in types  text ,  search ,  url ,  tel ,  email  and  number , are displayed in a drop-down menu when user clicks or double-clicks on the control.

Typically the right side of a control will also have an arrow pointing to the presence of predefined values.

html

Date and Time types

The types  month ,  week ,  date ,  time  and  datetime-local  can show an interface that allows a convenient selection of a date and time.

Predefined values can be shown there, allowing the user to quickly fill the control value.

Note:

When these types are not supported, a basic   type will be rendered instead, creating a text field. That field will correctly recognize recommended values and display them to the user in a drop-down menu.

html

Range type

When   attributes are included on   elements provided for a datalist associated with a  range  input type, they will be shown as a series of tick marks that the user can easily select.

html

Note:

The   attribute is intended to provide labels for tick marks, as defined in the  HTML Standard . However, current browser support varies; labels might not be displayed visually or as tooltips.

Color type

The  color  type can show predefined colors in a browser-provided interface.

html

title: <meter>: The HTML Meter element
content:
<meter>: The HTML Meter element

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since March 2017.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element represents either a scalar value within a known range or a fractional value.

Try it

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

The current numeric value. This must be between the minimum and maximum values (  attribute and   attribute) if they are specified. If unspecified or malformed, the value is  . If specified, but not within the range given by the   attribute and   attribute, the value is equal to the nearest end of the range.

Note:

Unless the   attribute is between   and   (inclusive), the   and   attributes should define the range so that the   attribute's value is within it.

The lower numeric bound of the measured range. This must be less than the maximum value (  attribute), if specified. If unspecified, the minimum value is  .

The upper numeric bound of the measured range. This must be greater than the minimum value (  attribute), if specified. If unspecified, the maximum value is  .

The upper numeric bound of the low end of the measured range. This must be greater than the minimum value (  attribute), and it also must be less than the high value and maximum value (  attribute and   attribute, respectively), if any are specified. If unspecified, or if less than the minimum value, the   value is equal to the minimum value.

The lower numeric bound of the high end of the measured range. This must be less than the maximum value (  attribute), and it also must be greater than the low value and minimum value (  attribute and   attribute, respectively), if any are specified. If unspecified, or if greater than the maximum value, the   value is equal to the maximum value.

This attribute indicates the optimal numeric value. It must be within the range (as defined by the   attribute and   attribute). When used with the   attribute and   attribute, it gives an indication where along the range is considered preferable. For example, if it is between the   attribute and the   attribute, then the lower range is considered preferred. The browser may color the meter's bar differently depending on whether the value is less than or equal to the optimum value.

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since October 2018.

The    HTML  element is a container element into which a site or app can inject the results of a calculation or the outcome of a user action.

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

A space-separated list of other elements'  s, indicating that those elements contributed input values to (or otherwise affected) the calculation.

The   element to associate the output with (its  form owner ). The value of this attribute must be the   of a   in the same document. (If this attribute is not set, the   is associated with its ancestor   element, if any.)

This attribute lets you associate   elements to  s anywhere in the document, not just inside a  . It can also override an ancestor   element. The   element's name and content are not submitted when the form is submitted.

The element's name. Used in the   API.

The   value, name, and contents are NOT submitted during form submission.

Accessibility

Many browsers implement this element as an   region. Assistive technology will thereby announce the results of UI interactions posted inside it without requiring that focus is switched away from the controls that produce those results.

Examples

In the following example, the form provides a slider whose value can range between   and  , and an   element into which you can enter a second number. The two numbers are added together, and the result is displayed in the   element each time the value of any of the controls changes.

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since July 2015.

The    HTML  element displays an indicator showing the completion progress of a task, typically displayed as a progress bar.

Try it

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

This attribute describes how much work the task indicated by the   element requires. The   attribute, if present, must have a value greater than   and be a valid floating point number. The default value is  .

This attribute specifies how much of the task that has been completed. It must be a valid floating point number between   and  , or between   and   if   is omitted. If there is no   attribute, the progress bar is indeterminate; this indicates that an activity is ongoing with no indication of how long it is expected to take.

Note:

Unlike the   element, the minimum value is always 0, and the   attribute is not allowed for the   element.

Note:

The   pseudo-class can be used to match against indeterminate progress bars. To change the progress bar to indeterminate after giving it a value you must remove the value attribute with  .

Accessibility

Labelling

In most cases you should provide an accessible label when using  . While you can use the standard ARIA labelling attributes   or   as you would for any element with  , when using   you can alternatively use the   element.

Note:

Text placed between the element's tags is not an accessible label, it is only recommended as a fallback for old browsers that do not support this element.

If the   element is describing the loading progress of a section of a page, use   to point to the status, and set   on the section that is being updated, removing the   attribute when it has finished loading.

content:
<selectedcontent>: The selected option display element

Limited availability

This feature is not Baseline because it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers.

Learn more

Report feedback

Experimental:   This is an  experimental technology

Check the  Browser compatibility table  carefully before using this in production.

The    HTML  is used inside a   element to display the contents of its currently selected   within its first child  . This enables you to style all parts of a   element, referred to as " customizable selects ".

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

Description

You use the   element as the only child of a   element, which must be the first child of the   element. Any   element, the only other valid child of  , must come after the   and nested   pair.

html

How   works behind the scenes

The   element contains a clone of the content of the currently selected  . The browser renders this clone using  . When the selected   changes, such as during a   event, the contents of   are replaced with a clone of the newly selected  . Being aware of this behavior is important, especially when working with dynamic content.

Warning:

Since the browser updates   only when the selected   changes, any dynamic modifications to the content of the selected   after the   is rendered won't be cloned to  . You'll need to update   manually. Watch out if you're using any of the popular front-end JavaScript frameworks where   elements are updated dynamically after the initial render–the result may not match what you expect in  .

inertness

By default, any   inside a   element is  inert . As a result, all content inside that button—including  —is also inert.

This means users can't interact with or focus on content inside  .

Styling the selected option's content with CSS

You can target the content of the currently selected   element and style how it appears inside the select button. Styling the button doesn't affect the styling of the content of the   that was cloned. This lets you customize how the selected option appears in the button, separately from how it appears in the drop-down list.

For example, your   elements may contain icons, images, or even videos that render nicely inside the drop-down, but could cause the select   to increase in size, look untidy, and affect the surrounding layout. By targeting the content inside  , you can hide elements such as images in the button, without affecting how they appear in the drop-down, as shown in the following snippet:

css

Note:

If the   and/or   elements are not included inside  , the browser creates an implicit   to display the contents of the selected  . This fallback button cannot be targeted with CSS using the   or   type selector.

Examples

You can see a full example that includes the   element in our  Customizable select elements  guide.

title: <details>: The Details disclosure element
content:
<details>: The Details disclosure element

Baseline

Widely available

*

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since January 2020.

* Some parts of this feature may have varying levels of support.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element creates a disclosure widget in which information is visible only when the widget is toggled into an open state. A summary or label must be provided using the   element.

A disclosure widget is typically presented onscreen using a small triangle that rotates (or twists) to indicate open/closed state, with a label next to the triangle. The contents of the   element are used as the label for the disclosure widget. The contents of the   provide the  accessible description  for the  .

Try it

A   widget can be in one of two states. The default  closed  state displays only the triangle and the label inside   (or a  user agent -defined default string if no  ).

When the user clicks on the widget or focuses it then presses the space bar, it "twists" open, revealing its contents. The common use of a triangle which rotates or twists around to represent opening or closing the widget is why these are sometimes called "twisty".

You can use CSS to style the disclosure widget, and you can programmatically open and close the widget by setting/removing its   attribute. Unfortunately, at this time, there's no built-in way to animate the transition between open and closed.

By default when closed, the widget is only tall enough to display the disclosure triangle and summary. When open, it expands to display the details contained within.

Fully standards-compliant implementations automatically apply the CSS   to the   element. You can use this or the   pseudo-element to  customize the disclosure widget .

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

This Boolean attribute indicates whether the details — that is, the contents of the   element — are currently visible. The details are shown when this attribute exists, or hidden when this attribute is absent. By default this attribute is absent which means the details are not visible.

Note:

You have to remove this attribute entirely to make the details hidden.   makes the details visible because this attribute is Boolean.

This attribute enables multiple   elements to be connected, with only one open at a time. This allows developers to easily create UI features such as accordions without scripting.

The   attribute specifies a group name — give multiple   elements the same   value to group them. Only one of the grouped   elements can be open at a time — opening one will cause another to close. If multiple grouped   elements are given the   attribute, only the first one in the source order will be rendered open.

Note:

elements don't have to be adjacent to one another in the source to be part of the same group.

Events

In addition to the usual events supported by HTML elements, the   element supports the   event, which is dispatched to the   element whenever its state changes between open and closed. It is sent  after  the state is changed, although if the state changes multiple times before the browser can dispatch the event, the events are coalesced so that only one is sent.

You can use an event listener for the   event to detect when the widget changes state:

js

Examples

A basic disclosure example

This example shows a basic   element with a  .

html

Result

Creating an open disclosure box

To start the   box in its open state, add the Boolean   attribute:

html

Result

Multiple named disclosure boxes

We include several   boxes, all with the same name so only one can be open at a time:

html

Result

Try opening all the disclosure widgets. When you open one, all the others automatically close.

Customizing the appearance

Now let's apply some CSS to customize the appearance of the disclosure box.

CSS

css

This CSS creates a look similar to a tabbed interface, where clicking the tab opens it to reveal its contents.

Note:

In browsers that don't support the   pseudo-class, you can use the attribute selector   to style the   element when it is in the open state.

HTML

html

Result

See the   page for an  example of customizing the disclosure widget .

flow content .

title: <summary>: The Disclosure Summary element
content:
<summary>: The Disclosure Summary element

Baseline

Widely available

*

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since January 2020.

* Some parts of this feature may have varying levels of support.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element specifies a summary, caption, or legend for a   element's disclosure box. Clicking the   element toggles the state of the parent   element open and closed.

Try it

Attributes

This element only includes the  global attributes .

Usage notes

The   element's contents can be any heading content, plain text, or HTML that can be used within a paragraph.

A   element may  only  be used as the first child of a   element. When the user clicks on the summary, the parent   element is toggled open or closed, and then a   event is sent to the   element, which can be used to let you know when this state change occurs.

The content of the   provides the  accessible description  for the  .

Default label text

If a   element's first child is not a   element, the  user agent  will use a default string (typically "Details") as the label for the disclosure box.

Default style

Per the HTML specification, the default style for   elements includes  . This makes it possible to change or remove the icon displayed as the disclosure widget next to the label from the default, which is typically a triangle.

You can also change the style to   to remove the disclosure triangle.

See the  Browser compatibility  section for details, as not all browsers support full functionality of this element yet.

For WebKit-based browsers, such as Safari, it is possible to control the icon display through the non-standard CSS pseudo-element  . To remove the disclosure triangle, use  .

Examples

Below are some examples showing   in use. You can find more examples in the documentation for the   element.

Basic example

A basic example showing the use of   in a   element:

html

Result

Summaries as headings

You can use heading elements in  , like this:

html

Result

This currently has some spacing issues that could be addressed using CSS.

Warning:

The role assigned to the   element varies across browsers. Some still assign it a default   role, which removes all roles from its children. This inconsistency can cause issues for users of assistive technologies such as screen readers (  in the previous example will have its role removed and will not be treated as a heading for these users). You should test your   implementation on multiple platforms to ensure there's consistent accessibility support.

HTML in summaries

This example adds some semantics to the   element to indicate the label as important:

html

Result

Changing the summary's icon

The   element's marker, the disclosure triangle, can be customized with CSS. The marker can be targeted using the   pseudo-element. which accepts the   shorthand property and its longhand component properties, such as  . This enables changing the triangle to an image (usually with  ) or a string (including emojis). In this example, we replace the content of one disclosure widget and remove the icon from another by setting   before adding a custom disclosure icon via generated content.

CSS

In the first disclosure widget, we style the  , changing the   based on the   element's   attribute. For the second widget, we remove the marker with   properties, then add styled generated content with the   pseudo-element. We also include styles for   to target Safari. The selector for the browser-specific pseudo-element is included in an   pseudo-class so it doesn't invalidate the selector list.

css

The CSS includes the    attribute selector , matching only when the   attribute is present (when the   are open). The   and   pseudo-classes target the first and sibling elements of the same type. We included the prefixed   pseudo-element within a   pseudo-class as it takes a  forgiving selector list , so if the prefixed pseudo-element is invalid in a browser, the whole selector block will not be invalid. We also used CSS  nesting . See the  CSS selectors  module.

title: <dialog>: The Dialog element
content:
<dialog>: The Dialog element

Baseline

Widely available

*

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since March 2022.

* Some parts of this feature may have varying levels of support.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element represents a modal or non-modal dialog box or other interactive component, such as a dismissible alert, inspector, or subwindow.

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

Warning:

The   attribute must not be used on the   element. See  Additional notes .

Specifies the types of user actions that can be used to close the   element. This attribute distinguishes three methods by which a dialog might be closed:

A  light dismiss user action , in which the   is closed when the user clicks or taps outside it. This is equivalent to the  "light dismiss" behavior of "auto" state popovers .

A  platform-specific user action , such as pressing the  Esc  key on desktop platforms, or a "back" or "dismiss" gesture on mobile platforms.

A developer-specified mechanism such as a   with a   handler that invokes   or a   submission.

Possible values are:

The dialog can be dismissed using any of the three methods.

The dialog can be dismissed with a platform-specific user action or a developer-specified mechanism.

The dialog can only be dismissed with a developer-specified mechanism.

If the   element does not have a valid   value specified, then

if it was opened using  , it behaves as if the value was

otherwise, it behaves as if the value was  .

Indicates that the dialog box is active and is available for interaction. If the   attribute is not set, the dialog box will not be visible to the user.

It is recommended to use the   or   method to render dialogs, rather than the   attribute. If a   is opened using the   attribute, it is non-modal.

Note:

While you can toggle between the open and closed states of non-modal dialog boxes by toggling the presence of the   attribute, this approach is not recommended. See   for more information.

Description

The HTML   element is used to create both modal and non-modal dialog boxes.

Modal dialog boxes block interaction with other UI elements, making the rest of the page  inert , while non-modal dialog boxes allow interaction with the rest of the page.

Controlling dialogs using JavaScript

JavaScript can be used to display and close the   element.

You can use the   method to display a modal dialog and the   method to display a non-modal dialog. The dialog box can be closed using the   method or using the   method when submitting a   that is nested within the   element.

Modal dialogs can also be closed by pressing the  Esc  key.

Modal dialogs using invoker commands

Modal dialogs can be declaratively opened and closed using the  Invoker Commands API  HTML attributes   and  , which can be set on   elements.

The   attribute sets the particular command that is to be sent when the   element is clicked, while   sets the   of the target dialog.

The commands that can be sent for dialogs are  ,  , and  .

The HTML below demonstrates how to apply the attributes to a   element so it can be pressed to open a modal   with an   of "my-dialog".

html

Non-modal dialogs using popover commands

Non-modal dialogs can be declaratively opened, closed, and toggled using the  Popover API  HTML attributes   and  , which can be defined on   and   elements.

The   must be turned into a popover by adding the   attribute.

You can then use   on a button/input to indicate the target popover, and   to specify the action to occur on the popover when the button is clicked.

Note that, because the dialog is a popover, it will be non-modal, so you can close it by clicking outside the dialog.

The HTML below shows how to apply the attributes to a   element so it can be pressed to show and hide a modal   with an   of "my-dialog".

html

The Popover API also provides properties that can be used to get and set the state in JavaScript.

Closing dialogs

It is important to provide a closing mechanism for every   element, and to ensure that this works on devices that might not have a physical keyboard.

There are numerous ways to close a dialog:

Submitting the form within the   element with   set on the   element (see the  Using the dialog open attribute  example).

Clicking outside the dialog area when "light dismiss" is enabled (see the  Popover API HTML attributes  example).

Pressing the  Esc  key, in dialogs where it is enabled (see the  Popover API HTML attributes  example).

Calling the   method (see the  modal example ).

CSS Styling

A   can be selected using its element name (like any other element), and you can also match its state using pseudo-classes such as   and  .

The CSS   pseudo-element can be used to style the backdrop of a modal dialog, which is displayed behind the   element when the dialog is displayed using the   method.

This pseudo-element could be used, for example, to blur, darken, or otherwise obfuscate the inert content behind the modal dialog.

Additional notes

HTML   elements can be used to close a dialog box if they have the attribute   or if the button used to submit the form has   set. When a   within a   is submitted via the   method, the dialog box closes, the states of the form controls are saved but not submitted, and the   property gets set to the value of the button that was activated.

The   attribute should be added to the element the user is expected to interact with immediately upon opening a modal dialog. If no other element involves more immediate interaction, it is recommended to add   to the close button inside the dialog, or the dialog itself if the user is expected to click/activate it to dismiss.

Do not add the   property to the   element as it is not interactive and does not receive focus. The dialog's contents, including the close button contained in the dialog, can receive focus and be interactive.

Accessibility

When implementing a dialog, it is important to consider the most appropriate place to set user focus. When using   to open a  , focus is set on the first nested focusable element. Explicitly indicating the initial focus placement by using the   attribute will help ensure initial focus is set on the element deemed the best initial focus placement for any particular dialog. When in doubt, as it may not always be known where initial focus could be set within a dialog, particularly for instances where a dialog's content is dynamically rendered when invoked, the   element itself may provide the best initial focus placement.

Ensure a mechanism is provided to allow users to close the dialog. The most robust way to ensure that all users can close the dialog is to include an explicit button to do so, such as a confirmation, cancellation, or close button.

By default, a dialog invoked by the   method can be dismissed by pressing the  Esc  key. A non-modal dialog does not dismiss via the  Esc  key by default, and depending on what the non-modal dialog represents, it may not be desired for this behavior. Keyboard users expect the  Esc  key to close modal dialogs; ensure that this behavior is implemented and maintained. If multiple modal dialogs are open, pressing the  Esc  key should close only the last shown dialog. When using  , this behavior is provided by the browser.

While dialogs can be created using other elements, the native   element provides usability and accessibility features that must be replicated if you use other elements for a similar purpose. If you're creating a custom dialog implementation, ensure that all expected default behaviors are supported and proper labeling recommendations are followed.

The   element is exposed by browsers in a manner similar to custom dialogs that use the ARIA  role="dialog"  attribute.   elements invoked by the   method implicitly have  aria-modal="true" , whereas   elements invoked by the   method or displayed using the   attribute or by changing the default   of a   are exposed as  . When implementing modal dialogs, everything other than the   and its contents should be rendered inert using the   attribute. When using   along with the   method, this behavior is provided by the browser.

Examples

Invoker Command API HTML attributes

This example demonstrates how you can use open and close a modal dialog using the   and   HTML attributes of the  Invoker Commands API .

First, we declare a   element, setting the   attribute to  , and the   attribute to the   of the dialog to open ( ).

Then we declare a   element that contains a "Close"  . This button sends the   command to the (same) dialog id.

html

Result

Open the dialog by pressing the "Open dialog" button.

You can close the dialog by selecting the "Close" button or pressing the  Esc  key.

Popover API HTML attributes

This example demonstrates how you can open and close a non-modal dialog using the  ,  , and   HTML attributes of the  Popover API .

The   is turned into a popover by adding the   attribute.

Since we haven't specified a value for the attribute, the default value of   is used.

This enables "light dismiss" behavior, allowing the dialog to be closed by clicking outside the dialog or by pressing  Esc .

We could instead have set   to disable "light dismiss" behavior, in which case the dialog would have to be closed using the "Close" button.

Note that we haven't specified the   attribute for the   that opens the dialog.

It isn't needed in this case, because its default value is  , which will toggle the dialog between its open and closed states when the button is clicked.

html

Result

Open the dialog by pressing the "Open dialog" button.

You can close the dialog by selecting the "Close" button or pressing the  Esc  key.

You can also close it by selecting outside the dialog, as it is non-modal.

Using the dialog   attribute

This example demonstrates how you can set the boolean   attribute on a   element, in order to create an HTML-only non-modal dialog that is already open when the page loads.

The dialog can be closed by clicking the "OK" button because the   attribute in the   element is set to  .

In this case, no JavaScript is needed to close the form.

html

Result

This dialog is initially open and non-modal because of the presence of the   attribute.

After clicking "OK", the dialog gets dismissed, leaving the Result frame empty.

Note:

Reload the page to reset the output.

When the dialog is dismissed, there is no method provided to reopen it. The preferred method to display non-modal dialogs is to use the   method.

It is possible to toggle the display of the dialog by adding or removing the boolean   attribute, but this is not the recommended practice.

Creating a modal dialog

This example demonstrates a modal dialog with a  gradient  backdrop. The   method opens the modal dialog when the "Show the dialog" button is activated. The dialog can be closed by pressing the  Esc  key or via the   method when the "Close" button within the dialog is activated.

When a dialog opens, the browser, by default, gives focus to the first element that can be focused within the dialog. In this example, the   attribute is applied to the "Close" button, giving it focus when the dialog opens, as this is the element we expect the user will interact with immediately after the dialog opens.

HTML

html

CSS

We can style the backdrop of the dialog by using the   pseudo-element.

css

JavaScript

The dialog is opened modally using the   method and closed using the   or   methods.

js

Result

When the modal dialog is displayed, it appears above any other dialogs that might be present. Everything outside the modal dialog is inert and interactions outside the dialog are blocked. Notice that when the dialog is open, with the exception of the dialog itself, interaction with the document is not possible; the "Show the dialog" button is mostly obfuscated by the almost opaque backdrop of the dialog and is inert.

Handling the return value from the dialog

This example demonstrates the   of the   element and how to close a modal dialog by using a form. By default, the   is the empty string or the value of the button that submits the form within the   element, if there is one.

This example opens a modal dialog when the "Show the dialog" button is activated. The dialog contains a form with a   and two   elements, which default to  . An event listener updates the value of the "Confirm" button when the select option changes. If the "Confirm" button is activated to close the dialog, the current value of the button is the return value. If the dialog is closed by pressing the "Cancel" button, the   is  .

When the dialog is closed, the return value is displayed under the "Show the dialog" button. If the dialog is closed by pressing the  Esc  key, the   is not updated, and the   event doesn't occur, so the text in the   is not updated.

HTML

html

JavaScript

The dialog is opened using using an event listener on the "Show the dialog" button, which calls   when the button is clicked.

The dialog is closed when the "Cancel" button is clicked, because the   includes the   attribute.

When a form's method is  , the state of the form is saved but not submitted, and the dialog gets closed (the attribute overrides the  's default   method).

Without an  , submitting the form via the default   method causes a page to reload.

We use JavaScript to prevent the submission and close the dialog with the   and   methods, respectively.

js

Result

Closing a dialog with a required form input

When a form inside a dialog has a required input, the user agent will only let you close the dialog once you provide a value for the required input. To close such dialog, either use the   attribute on the close button or call the   method on the dialog object when the close button is clicked.

html

JavaScript

js

Result

From the output, we see it is impossible to close the dialog using the  Normal close  button. But the dialog can be closed if we bypass the form validation using the   attribute on the  Cancel  button. Programmatically,   will also close such dialog.

Comparison of different closedby behaviors

This example demonstrates the difference in behavior between different values of the   attribute.

HTML

We provide three   elements and three   elements. Each button will be programmed to open a different dialog that demonstrates the behavior of one of the three values of the   attribute —  ,  , and  . Note that each   element contains a   element that will be used to close it.

html

JavaScript

Here we assign different variables to reference the main control   elements, the   elements, and the "Close"   elements inside the dialogs. First we assign a   event listener to each control button using  , the event handler function of which opens the associated   element via  . We then loop through the "Close"   references, assigning each one a   event handler function that closes its   element via  .

js

Result

The rendered result is as follows:

Try clicking each button to open a dialog. The first one can only be closed by clicking its "Close" button. The second one can also be closed via a device-specific user action such as pressing the  Esc  key. The third one has full  "light-dismiss" behavior , so it can also be closed by clicking or tapping outside the dialog.

Animating dialogs

s are set to   when hidden and   when shown, as well as being removed from / added to the  top layer  and the  accessibility tree . Therefore, for   elements to be animated the   property needs to be animatable.  Supporting browsers  animate   with a variation on the  discrete animation type . Specifically, the browser will flip between   and another value of   so that the animated content is shown for the entire animation duration.

So for example:

When animating   from   to   (or another visible   value), the value will flip to   at   of the animation duration so it is visible throughout.

When animating   from   (or another visible   value) to  , the value will flip to   at   of the animation duration so it is visible throughout.

Note:

When animating using  CSS transitions ,   needs to be set to enable the above behavior. This behavior is available by default when animating with  CSS animations ; an equivalent step is not required.

Transitioning dialog elements

When animating  s with CSS transitions, the following features are required:

at-rule

Provides a set of starting values for properties set on the   that you want to transition from every time it is opened. This is needed to avoid unexpected behavior. By default, CSS transitions only occur when a property changes from one value to another on a visible element; they are not triggered on elements' first style updates, or when the   type changes from   to another type.

property

Add   to the transitions list so that the   will remain as   (or another visible   value set on the dialog's open state) for the duration of the transition, ensuring the other transitions are visible.

property

Include   in the transitions list to ensure the removal of the   from the top layer is deferred until the transition completes, again ensuring the transition is visible.

property

Set   on the   and   transitions (or on the   shorthand) to enable discrete transitions on these two properties that are not by default animatable.

Here is a quick example to show what this might look like.

HTML

The HTML contains a   element, plus a button to show the dialog. Additionally, the   element contains another button to close itself.

html

CSS

In the CSS, we include a   block that defines the transition starting styles for the   and   properties, transition end styles on the   state, and default styles on the default   state to transition back to once the   has appeared. Note how the  's   list includes not only these properties, but also the   and   properties, each with   set on them.

We also set a starting style value for the   property on the   that appears behind the   when it opens, to provide a nice darkening animation. The   selector selects only the backdrops of   elements when the dialog is open.

css

Note:

In browsers that don't support the   pseudo-class, you can use the attribute selector   to style the   element when it is in the open state.

JavaScript

The JavaScript adds event handlers to the show and close buttons causing them to show and close the   when they are clicked:

js

Result

The code renders as follows:

Note:

Because  s change from   to   each time they are shown, the   transitions from its   styles to its   styles every time the entry transition occurs. When the   closes, it transitions from its   state to the default   state.

It is possible for the style transition on entry and exit to be different in such cases. See our  Demonstration of when starting styles are used  example for a proof of this.

dialog keyframe animations

When animating a   with CSS keyframe animations, there are some differences to note from transitions:

You don't provide a  .

You include the   value in a keyframe; this will be the   value for the entirety of the animation, or until another non-  display value is encountered.

You don't need to explicitly enable discrete animations; there is no equivalent to   inside keyframes.

You don't need to set   inside keyframes either; the   animation handles the animation of the   from shown to hidden.

Let's have a look at an example so you can see what this looks like.

HTML

First, the HTML contains a   element, plus a button to show the dialog. Additionally, the   element contains another button to close itself.

html

CSS

The CSS defines keyframes to animate between the closed and shown states of the  , plus the fade-in animation for the  's backdrop. The   animations include animating   to make sure the actual visible animation effects remain visible for the whole duration. Note that it wasn't possible to animate the backdrop fade out — the backdrop is immediately removed from the DOM when the   is closed, so there is nothing to animate.

css

JavaScript

Finally, the JavaScript adds event handlers to the buttons to enable showing and closing the  :

js

title: <geolocation>: The geolocation element
content:
<geolocation>: The geolocation element

Experimental:   This is an  experimental technology

Check the  Browser compatibility table  carefully before using this in production.

The    HTML  element creates an interactive control for the user to share their location data with the page.

It provides:

An intuitive browser-defined UI.

A process for handling the necessary permissions for the   feature.

API features for accessing location data and responding to received location data and permission changes.

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

Experimental

A boolean attribute that, when set to  , specifies that the browser should immediately retrieve location data when the   element is rendered, provided permission was previously granted. If set to  , location data is not retrieved until the user activates the control. Defaults to  .

If permission was not previously granted, this attribute has no effect.

Experimental

A boolean attribute that, when set to  , specifies that the browser should retrieve location data whenever the position of the user's device changes. If set to  , location data is only retrieved once. Defaults to  .

Description

The   element provides a declarative browser-defined control for sharing location data. In Chrome, for example, the button features a "map pin" icon and intuitive text ("Use location" in English content).

It also allows for intuitive management of user permissions.

For example, in Chrome, if the user previously denied permission to access location data, or dismissed the permission dialog without making a choice, they are free to press the button again to update their choice.

In cases where they previously denied permission, subsequent dialogs will inform them that they previously didn't allow location data to be shared, and ask them whether they want to continue not allowing it, or to allow it.

A key aspect of the   element is that it reflects the user's conscious choice, and blocks possible usage that might trick the user into providing their location data unwittingly (see   blocking  for more information).

The element's DOM API interface,  , provides features to access returned position data, current permission status, and errors if the data retrieval was unsuccessful, reducing the amount of JavaScript logic that needs to be written. It also has events available to run code in response to location data being received, changes in permission status, and user interactions with the permission dialog.

Note:

For performance reasons, a maximum of three   elements are allowed on any one page. If this quota is exceeded, all   elements have their functionality disabled.

Relationship with the Geolocation API

The  Geolocation API  provides an older alternative for handling location data. This API has some shortcomings that the   element aims to solve, most notably that the UI and underlying logic for requesting the data needs to be implemented from scratch each time, and the handling of permissions can be unintuitive.

The   element uses features of the Geolocation API in the background. By default, the browser requests location data once, as if the   method was called. However, if the   attribute is set to  , the browser updates the data whenever the device position changes, as if   was called.

If data is successfully retrieved, it is available in the   property, which contains a   object. If data retrieval is unsuccessful, error information is available in the   property, which contains a   object.

Setting the button language

The global   attribute is used by the   element to select a language for its rendered text. This means that you can set a   attribute directly on the   element or on one of its ancestors to tell the browser what language to use for the button label.

If no suitable   attribute is set, the browser's preferred language setting is used.

Including fallback content

You can include fallback content between the   element's opening and closing tags that will be displayed if it isn't supported. For example, you might include a "Not supported" message:

html

However, a better real-world solution might be to include a regular   element that uses the Geolocation API to retrieve location data:

html

blocking

One key idea behind the design of the   element is that it should reflect a user's conscious choice to expose position information, and prevent bad actors from tricking users into activating it, for example via  clickjacking . Because of this, the browser keeps a record of so-called  blocker reasons  for each rendered element.

When a blocker is active on a   element, it is prevented from functioning (blocked), either temporarily or permanently, depending on the reason. When a   element is blocked, it is said to be invalid. You can check whether it is invalid by querying the   property. You can also return the reason why it is invalid via the   property — see that page for a full list of possible reasons.

Styling restrictions

The   element has several constraints on the CSS styles that can be applied to it. Some of these constraints are designed to enforce fundamental accessibility, and will result in the button being deactivated if they are not adhered to. Some enforce certain values or value ranges for various properties.

Any properties that are not listed in the following sub-sections, or logically equivalent to a physical property listed in the following sub-sections, are ignored when set on the   element.

Accessibility restrictions

The rendered   button is deactivated (meaning that pressing it will have no effect) if the following constraints are not adhered to:

The  color contrast  ratio between   and   must be at least 3:1.

The   must not be smaller than the   value (in the case of keyword values), or its computed value (in the case of other value types).

Value constraints

The following CSS property value constraints are applied to the   element. If an attempt is made to set these properties to values outside the listed constraints on the   element, the value is adjusted to equal the constraint (in the case of an exact value constraint) or to equal to nearest computed value upper or lower bound (in the case of a range constraint).

or greater.

or greater.

Between   and  , inclusive.

Between   and  , inclusive.

Between   and  , inclusive.

or greater.

or less.   is an accepted value.

The computed value of   or less.

or less.

Complex constraints

The following constraints are more complex than simple value constraints:

Block direction padding

If the   is set to  , the   and   (and equivalent physical properties for the current  writing mode ) are constrained to a maximum of   and must be equal.

Inline direction padding

If the   is set to  , the   and   (and equivalent physical properties for the current  writing mode ) are constrained to a maximum of   and must be equal.

Properties that can be set normally

The following CSS properties can be used normally:

,  ,  ,  , and

,  , and

,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  , and

Accessibility

The   element has an accessible name written in the  language it is set to . It also has a   of   so that it is recognized as a button by screen readers.

In addition, the   element has a default   value of  , so it behaves like a real   with respect to keyboard focus.

Finally, refer back to the  Accessibility restrictions  section for information on styling constraints applied to the   element to enforce fundamental accessibility requirements.

Examples

Basic usage example

This example uses the   element to retrieve your current location, which is printed out below the button in a   element. The example also uses a regular   fallback to retrieve the location data in non-supporting browsers.

HTML

We include a   element with a   fallback nested inside it, which will be rendered in browsers that do not support  . We also include a   to output location data and errors into.

html

JavaScript

In our script, we start off by grabbing a reference to the output   element. We then detect whether the   element is supported by testing  :

If it is supported, we first grab a reference to the   element and then add a   event listener.

When the button is pressed and the data is retrieved, the listener prints the (lat, long) coordinates to the output   (retrieved via the   property), or an error message if the data retrieval was unsuccessful (retrieved via the   property).

If it isn't supported, we grab a reference to the fallback   element and retrieve and print the same data, except that this time we are using a   event listener on the button, and a   call to retrieve the data.

js

Result

See this code  running live  ( source code ). You can also find a version of this example that includes the   attribute on the   element and therefore fetches location data each time the user's device position changes (see it  running live , and the  source code ).

Try viewing the demos in a supported browser and an unsupported browser if possible, and note the difference in permissions dialog flow when you choose to allow or deny permission to use  .

For a walkthrough of a more complete example that uses location data to create a map of your local area, see the   reference page.

title: <slot>: The Web Component Slot element
content:
<slot>: The Web Component Slot element

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since January 2020.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element—part of the  Web Components  technology suite—is a placeholder inside a web component that you can fill with your own markup, which lets you create separate DOM trees and present them together.

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

The slot's name. When the slot's containing component gets rendered, the slot is rendered with the custom element's child that has a matching   attribute. A  named slot  is a   element with a   attribute. Unnamed slots have the name default to the empty string. Names should be unique per shadow root: if you have two slots with the same name, all of the elements with a matching   attribute will be assigned to the first slot with that name.

Examples

html

Note:

You can see this complete example in action at  element-details  (see it  running live ). In addition, you can find an explanation at  Using templates and slots .

title: <template>: The Content Template element
content:
<template>: The Content Template element

Baseline

Widely available

*

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since November 2015.

* Some parts of this feature may have varying levels of support.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    HTML  element serves as a mechanism for holding  HTML  fragments, which can either be used later via JavaScript or generated immediately into shadow DOM.

Attributes

This element includes the  global attributes .

Creates a  shadow root  for the parent element.

It is a declarative version of the   method and accepts the same  enumerated  values.

Exposes the internal shadow root DOM for JavaScript (recommended for most use cases).

Hides the internal shadow root DOM from JavaScript.

Note:

The HTML parser creates a   object in the DOM for the first   in a node with this attribute set to an allowed value.

If the attribute is not set, or not set to an allowed value — or if a   has already been declaratively created in the same parent — then an   is constructed.

A   cannot subsequently be changed into a shadow root after parsing, for example, by setting  .

Note:

You may find the non-standard   attribute in older tutorials and examples that used to be supported in Chrome 90-110. This attribute has since been removed and replaced by the standard   attribute.

Sets the value of the   property of a   created using this element to  .

If set, a clone of the shadow host (the parent element of this  ) created with   or   will include a shadow root in the copy.

Sets the   property of a   created using this element to  , rather than the document's  custom element registry .

This allows a scoped   to be attached later using  .

Sets the value of the   property of a   created using this element to  .

If this is set and a non-focusable element in the shadow tree is selected, then focus is delegated to the first focusable element in the tree.

The value defaults to  .

Experimental

Non-standard

Sets the value of the   property of a   created using this element. The value should be the ID of an element inside the shadow DOM. If set, target references to the host element from outside the shadow DOM will cause the referenced target element to become the effective target of the reference to the host element.

Sets the value of the   property of a   created using this element to  .

If set, the shadow root may be serialized by calling the   or   methods with the   parameter set  .

The value defaults to  .

Usage notes

This element has no permitted content, because everything nested inside it in the HTML source does not actually become the children of the   element. The   property of the   element is always empty, and you can only access said nested content via the special   property. However, if you call   or similar methods on the   element, then you would be inserting children into the   element itself, which is a violation of its content model and does not actually update the   returned by the   property.

Due to the way the   element is parsed, all  ,  , and   opening and closing tags inside the template are syntax errors and are ignored by the parser, so   is the same as  .

There are two main ways to use the   element.

Template document fragment

By default, the element's content is not rendered.

The corresponding   interface includes a standard   property (without an equivalent content/markup attribute). This   property is read-only and holds a   that contains the DOM subtree represented by the template.

The   and   methods both create a copy of a node. The difference is that   clones the node in the context of the calling document, whereas   uses the document of the node being cloned. The document context determines the   for constructing any custom elements. For this reason, use   to clone the   fragment so that custom element descendants are constructed using the definitions in the current document, rather than the separate document that owns the template content. See the   page's examples for more details.

Note that the   container itself should not have data attached to it. See the  Data on the DocumentFragment is not cloned  example for more details.

Declarative Shadow DOM

If the   element contains the   attribute with a value of either   or  , the HTML parser will immediately generate a shadow DOM. The element is replaced in the DOM by its content wrapped in a  , which is attached to the parent element.

This is the declarative equivalent of calling   to attach a shadow root to an element.

If the element has any other value for  , or does not have the   attribute, the parser generates a  .

Similarly, if there are multiple declarative shadow roots, only the first one is replaced by a   — subsequent instances are parsed as   objects.

Examples

Generating table rows

First we start with the HTML portion of the example.

html

First, we have a table into which we will later insert content using JavaScript code. Then comes the template, which describes the structure of an HTML fragment representing a single table row.

Now that the table has been created and the template defined, we use JavaScript to insert rows into the table, with each row being constructed using the template as its basis.

js

The result is the original HTML table, with two new rows appended to it via JavaScript:

Implementing a declarative shadow DOM

In this example, a hidden support warning is included at the beginning of the markup. This warning is later set to be displayed via JavaScript if the browser doesn't support the   attribute. Next, there are two   elements, each containing nested   elements with different behaviors. The first   element is global to the whole document. The second one is scoped to the shadow root generated in place of the   element because of the presence of the   attribute.

html

js

Declarative Shadow DOM with delegated focus

This example demonstrates how   is applied to a shadow root that is created declaratively, and the effect this has on focus.

The code first declares a shadow root inside a   element, using the   element with the   attribute.

This displays both a non-focusable   containing text and a focusable   element.

It also uses CSS to style elements with   to blue, and to set the normal styling of the host element.

html

The second code block is identical except that it sets the   attribute, which delegates focus to the first focusable element in the tree if a non-focusable element in the tree is selected.

html

Last of all we use the following CSS to apply a red border to the parent   element when it has focus.

css

The results are shown below.

When the HTML is first rendered, the elements have no styling, as shown in the first image.

For the shadow root that does not have   set you can click anywhere except the   and the focus does not change (if you select the   element it will look like the second image).

For the shadow root with   set, clicking on the text (which is non-focusable) selects the   element, as this is the first focusable element in the tree.

This also focuses the parent element as shown below.

Data on the DocumentFragment is not cloned

When a   value is passed,   and similar methods move only the  child nodes  of that value into the target node. Therefore, it is usually preferable to attach event handlers to the children of a  , rather than to the   itself.

Consider the following HTML and JavaScript:

HTML

html

JavaScript

js

Result

Since   is a  , only its children are added to   when   is called; the event handlers of   are not copied. In contrast, because an event handler is added to the first  child node  of  , the event handler is copied when   is called, and clicking on it works as one would expect.

<acronym>

Deprecated:  This feature is no longer recommended. Though some browsers might still support it, it may have already been removed from the relevant web standards, may be in the process of being dropped, or may only be kept for compatibility purposes. Avoid using it, and update existing code if possible; see the  compatibility table  at the bottom of this page to guide your decision. Be aware that this feature may cease to work at any time.

The    HTML  element allows authors to clearly indicate a sequence of characters that compose an acronym or abbreviation for a word.

Warning:

Don't use this element. Use the   element instead.

Attributes

This element only has  global attributes , which are common to all elements.

DOM Interface

This element implements the   interface.

Examples

html

Result

Default styling

Though the purpose of this tag is purely for the convenience of the author, its default styling varies from one browser to another:

Opera, Firefox, Chrome, and some others add a dotted underline to the content of the element.

A few browsers not only add a dotted underline, but also put it in small caps; to avoid this styling, adding something like   in the CSS takes care of this case.

It is therefore recommended that web authors either explicitly style this element, or accept some cross-browser variation.

The    HTML  element defines a particular area in which another HTML document can be displayed. A frame should be used within a  .

Using the   element is not encouraged because of certain disadvantages such as performance problems and lack of accessibility for users with screen readers. Instead of the   element,   may be preferred.

Attributes

Like all other HTML elements, this element supports the  global attributes .

Deprecated

This attribute specifies the document that will be displayed by the frame.

Deprecated

This attribute is used for labeling frames. Without labeling, every link will open in the frame that it's in – the closest parent frame. See the   attribute for more information.

Deprecated

This attribute prevents resizing of frames by users.

Deprecated

This attribute defines the existence of a scrollbar. If this attribute is not used, the browser adds a scrollbar when necessary. There are two choices: "yes" for forcing a scrollbar even when it is not necessary and "no" for forcing no scrollbar even when it  is  necessary.

Deprecated

This attribute defines the height of the margin between frames.

Deprecated

This attribute defines the width of the margin between frames.

Deprecated

This attribute allows you to specify a frame's border.

Usage notes

Ruby annotations are for showing pronunciation of East Asian characters, like using Japanese furigana or Taiwanese bopomofo characters. The   element is used to separate out each segment of the ruby base text.

Even though   is not a  void element , it is common to just include the opening tag of each element in the source code, so that the ruby markup is less complex and easier to read. The browser can then fill in the full element in the rendered version.

You need to include one   element for each base segment/  element that you want to annotate.

The    HTML  element creates inline text which is presented using the  user agent's  default monospace font face. This element was created for the purpose of rendering text as it would be displayed on a fixed-width display such as a teletype, text-only screen, or line printer.

The terms  non-proportional ,  monotype , and  monospace  are used interchangeably and have the same general meaning: they describe a typeface whose characters are all the same number of pixels wide.

This element is obsolete, however. You should use the more semantically helpful  ,  ,  , or   elements for inline text that needs to be presented in monospace type, or the   tag for content that should be presented as a separate block.

Note:

If none of the semantic elements are appropriate for your use case (for example, if you need to show some content in a non-proportional font), you should consider using the   element, styling it as desired using CSS. The   property is a good place to start.

title: Using HTML comments <!-- … -->
content:
Using HTML comments <!-- … -->

An HTML  comment  is used to add explanatory notes to the markup or to prevent the browser from interpreting specific parts of the document.

Comments start with the string   and end with the string  , generally with text in between. This text cannot start with the string   or  , cannot contain the strings   or  , nor end with the string  , though   is allowed.

The browser ignores comments as it renders the code. In other words, they are not visible on the page - just in the code. HTML comments are a way for you to write helpful notes about your code or logic.

The above is true for  XML  comments as well. In addition, in XML, such as in  SVG  or  MathML  markup, a comment cannot contain the character sequence  .

Comments can be used on a single line, or span multiple lines. They can be used in the following places:

Before and after the  Doctype

Before and after the   element

As the content of most elements except:  ,  ,  ,  , because these elements interpret their content as raw text

Note:

While   elements should not have HTML comments and should use  JavaScript comments  instead, there was a legacy practice to enclose the whole script content in an HTML comment so ancient browsers that don't support JavaScript don't render it as text. This is now a  legacy feature of JavaScript itself  and you should not rely on it.

Syntax

html

Examples

html

Notes

HTML comments are only permitted as content. You cannot use it within a tag, such as before an HTML  attribute .

As with most programming languages that use the   comment syntax, comments cannot be nested. In other words, the first instance of   that follows an instance of   closes the comment.

While comments do start with a   and end in a  , a comment is not an HTML element.

Specifications

title: Using HTML form validation and the Constraint Validation API
content:
Using HTML form validation and the Constraint Validation API

The creation of web forms has always been a complex task. While marking up the form itself is easy, checking whether each field has a valid and coherent value is more difficult, and informing the user about the problem may become a headache.  HTML5  introduced new mechanisms for forms: it added new semantic types for the   element and  constraint validation  to ease the work of checking the form content on the client side. Basic, usual constraints can be checked, without the need for JavaScript, by setting new attributes; more complex constraints can be tested using the Constraint Validation API.

For a basic introduction to these concepts, with examples, see the  Form validation tutorial .

Note:

HTML Constraint validation doesn't remove the need for validation on the  server side . Even though far fewer invalid form requests are to be expected, invalid ones can still be sent in many ways:

By modifying HTML via the browser's developer tools.

By hand-crafting an HTTP request without using the form.

By programmatically writing content into the form (certain constraint validations are  only run  for user input, and not if you set the value of a form field using JavaScript).

Therefore, you should always validate form data on the server side, consistent with what is done on the client side.

Intrinsic and basic constraints

In HTML, basic constraints are declared in two ways:

By choosing the most semantically appropriate value for the   attribute of the   element, e.g., choosing the   type automatically creates a constraint that checks whether the value is a valid email address.

By setting values on validation-related attributes, allowing basic constraints to be described without the need for JavaScript.

Semantic input types

The intrinsic constraints for the   attribute are:

Input type

Constraint description

Associated violation

The value must be an absolute  URL , as defined in the  URL Living Standard .

TypeMismatch  constraint violation

The value must be a syntactically valid email address, which generally has the format   but can also be local such as  .

TypeMismatch  constraint violation

For the   input type, if the   attribute is set, several values can be set as a comma-separated list. If any of the values in the list do not satisfy the condition described here, the  Type mismatch  constraint violation is triggered.

Note that most input types don't have intrinsic constraints, as some are barred from constraint validation or have a sanitization algorithm transforming incorrect values to a correct default.

Validation-related attributes

In addition to the   attribute described above, the following attributes are used to describe basic constraints:

Attribute

Input types supporting the attribute

Possible values
